section_1_mohr_salt_preparation.overview: Mohr's salt (硫酸亚铁铵 / ferrous ammonium sulfate) has the formula (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O or (NH4)2SO4·FeSO4·6H2O, molecular weight 392.18. It is a double salt (复盐), crystallizing as transparent pale blue-green monoclinic crystals.

physical_properties.solubility: Soluble in water, insoluble in ethanol

FeSO4_7H2O_properties.melting_point: 64°C (melts in own crystal water), 400°C (decomposition of anhydrous)
FeSO4_7H2O_properties.dehydration: At 90°C heptahydrate loses water to colorless monohydrate; at 300°C loses all crystal water; decomposition at ~480°C: 2FeSO4 → Fe2O3 + SO2 + SO3
production: Steel pickling: Fe + H2SO4 → FeSO4 + H2
TiO2 production from ilmenite via sulfate process

standard_preparation_methods.FeSO4_1M_solution: Dissolve 278.01g FeSO4·7H2O in water with 10mL concentrated H2SO4, dilute to 1L. Final H2SO4 concentration ≈ 0.18 mol/L, pH ≈ 0.74.
standard_preparation_methods.Mohr_salt_0_1M_solution: Dissolve 39.213g (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O in water, add 10mL concentrated H2SO4, dilute to 1L. Final H2SO4 concentration ≈ 0.18 mol/L, pH ≈ 0.74.
standard_preparation_methods.note: Both standard solutions require addition of concentrated H2SO4 and must be prepared fresh before use (用时新配). Addition of a small iron nail to the reagent bottle is recommended to scavenge any Fe3+ via Fe + 2Fe3+ → 3Fe2+.

section_2_mohr_salt_stability_analysis.core_question: Why is Mohr's salt crystal more resistant to air oxidation than FeSO4·7H2O crystal?

theory_1_void_theory.name: 空隙说 (Void/Gap Theory)
theory_1_void_theory.claim: FeSO4·7H2O has larger crystal internal voids than Mohr's salt, allowing O2 molecules to penetrate and oxidize Fe2+.

refutation.calculation_error: Uses S6+ ionic radius (0.029nm) instead of SO42- thermochemical radius (0.230nm). When corrected: O2 diameter + [Fe(H2O)6]2+ radius + SO42- radius = 0.264 + 0.230 + 0.230 = 0.724nm, which is much larger than the measured Fe-S distance of 0.519nm.
refutation.density_calculation: Per-Fe2+ molar volume: Mohr's salt = 206 cm3/mol, FeSO4·7H2O = 146 cm3/mol. FeSO4·7H2O is actually MORE densely packed (only 71% of Mohr's salt volume per Fe). This contradicts the void theory.
refutation.physical_principle: Crystals are generally dense and impermeable. O2 molecules cannot freely pass through crystal surfaces into internal voids.

theory_1_void_theory.verdict: REFUTED - Uses incorrect ionic radii; density calculations disprove the claim.

theory_2_hydrogen_bond_theory.name: 氢键说 (Hydrogen Bond Network Theory)
theory_2_hydrogen_bond_theory.claim: Mohr's salt has more hydrogen bonds (from NH4+ ions) creating a stronger H-bond network that makes the crystal more compact and oxidation-resistant.

h_bond_energy_analysis.Mohr_salt_NH4_H_bonds: 8 N-H···:O bonds × 8 kJ/mol each = 64 kJ/mol total. NH4+ lacks lone pairs, cannot form O-H···:N bonds (stronger type at ~21 kJ/mol).
h_bond_energy_analysis.FeSO4_7H2O_h_bonds: 1 interstitial H2O forms 4 O-H···:O bonds × 21 kJ/mol each = 84 kJ/mol total.
h_bond_energy_analysis.conclusion: FeSO4·7H2O actually has STRONGER hydrogen bond energy (84 kJ/mol vs 64 kJ/mol).

refutation.structural_issue: NH4+ and [Fe(H2O)6]2+ are both cations and repel each other. They are separated by SO42- anions, so NH4+ H-bonds do not form a network involving [Fe(H2O)6]2+.

theory_2_hydrogen_bond_theory.verdict: REFUTED - H-bond energy calculation shows FeSO4·7H2O has stronger H-bonds; NH4+ cannot form a network with [Fe(H2O)6]2+.

theory_3_water_classification_theory.name: 结晶水分类理论 (Crystal Water Classification Theory)
theory_3_water_classification_theory.explanation: Based on the structural chemistry of salt hydrates (Zhou Gongdu, 1982), water molecules in crystalline hydrates fall into three categories:
theory_3_water_classification_theory.type_1_water: Coordinates to ONE metal ion via one coordinate bond. Relatively stable.
theory_3_water_classification_theory.type_2_water: Does NOT coordinate to any metal ion. Held only by hydrogen bonds. MOST UNSTABLE - easily lost with small activation energy.
theory_3_water_classification_theory.type_3_water: Coordinates to TWO metal ions simultaneously (bridging). MOST STABLE.

application_to_FeSO4_vs_Mohr.FeSO4_7H2O: 6 H2O coordinate to Fe2+ (Type 1), 1 H2O is interstitial - held only by H-bonds (Type 2). This 7th water is the weak point. It can easily lose H-bonds under low humidity (efflorescence), breaking the crystal structure and exposing [Fe(H2O)6]2+ to O2.
application_to_FeSO4_vs_Mohr.Mohr_salt: All 6 H2O coordinate directly to Fe2+ (all Type 1). NO Type 2 water. No weak H-bond-only water to lose. Crystal surface remains intact.
application_to_FeSO4_vs_Mohr.mechanism: Low humidity → Type 2 water H-bonds break in FeSO4·7H2O → surface crystal structure damaged → [Fe(H2O)6]2+ exposed → O2 oxidation → chain reaction of oxidation.

theory_3_water_classification_theory.experimental_prediction: If FeSO4·7H2O is placed in a sealed, O2-containing but water vapor-saturated environment, the crystal cannot effloresce, and oxidation should be prevented. Keep reagent bottles tightly sealed as the most effective preservation measure.
theory_3_water_classification_theory.verdict: ACCEPTED - Explains the structural basis consistently with known hydrate chemistry.

key_findings: Both FeSO4 and (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2 solutions are equally prone to oxidation. Standard analytical chemistry handbooks require both to be 'prepared fresh before use' (用时新配).
From Φ-pH diagram: O2 can oxidize Fe2+ at ANY pH. The O2/H2O line (E°=1.229V) is always above the Fe3+/Fe2+ line (E°=0.770V). ΔE = 0.458V.
At pH < 1.53, the oxidation tendency is somewhat smaller (the two lines are closer). Modern handbooks use standard acid addition to achieve pH ≈ 0.74.

NH4+ hydrolysis has NEGLIGIBLE effect on solution acidity: Fe2+ (pKa=6.74, Ka=1.8×10-7) is a much stronger acid than NH4+ (Ka=5.64×10-10). For 0.1M (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2, the pH calculated from Fe2+ hydrolysis is 3.87. At this acidity, NH4+ hydrolysis is strongly suppressed.

Ionic strength difference IS significant: (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2 solution has ionic strength ~1.75 times that of FeSO4 at equal concentration. Higher ionic strength → lower Fe2+ activity coefficient (reduced 'effective concentration') + lower O2 solubility → APPARENT slower oxidation. This may explain observations of Mohr's salt solutions oxidizing slightly slower.
standard_preservation_practices: Add sufficient acid (H2SO4 to ~0.18 mol/L, pH ~0.74)
Add a small iron nail to the reagent bottle: Fe + 2Fe3+ → 3Fe2+

section_4_cyanotype_chemistry.historical_context: Cyanotype (蓝晒) was invented by Sir John Herschel in 1842. It is one of the oldest photographic printing processes, producing characteristic Prussian blue images.

traditional_cyanotype.sensitizer: Ammonium iron(III) citrate (柠檬酸铁铵) + potassium ferricyanide (铁氰化钾 K3[Fe(CN)6])
traditional_cyanotype.photoreaction: UV/blue light photoreduces Fe(III)-citrate complex to Fe(II)
traditional_cyanotype.development: Water wash removes unreacted sensitizer, leaving blue image

ferrioxalate_cyanotype.sensitizer: Potassium tris(oxalato)ferrate(III) (三草酸合铁(III)酸钾 K3[Fe(C2O4)3]·3H2O) + potassium ferricyanide (optional)
ferrioxalate_cyanotype.photoreaction: Light-induced ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT): [Fe(C2O4)3]3- + hν → Fe2+ + C2O4·- + 2C2O42-
ferrioxalate_cyanotype.printing_reaction: Fe2+ reacts with [Fe(CN)6]3- to form Prussian blue, OR with excess oxalate to precipitate FeC2O4 (developable to Prussian blue with ferricyanide post-exposure)
ferrioxalate_cyanotype.quantum_yield: Quantum yield of Fe2+ production from ferrioxalate is ~1.0-1.2 across UV-visible range (basis of the Hatchard-Parker chemical actinometer)

key_formulations.classic_cyanotype_solution_A: 20g ammonium iron(III) citrate (green form) in 100mL water
key_formulations.working_solution: Mix equal volumes A + B, apply to paper in dim light, dry in dark
key_formulations.ferrioxalate_sensitizer: K3[Fe(C2O4)3]·3H2O aqueous solution (~0.1-0.5M), applied to paper and dried

exposure_and_processing.light_source: Sunlight (strong UV component), UV lamp (365nm), or artificial UV source

typical_exposure_times.direct_sunlight: 3-15 minutes depending on intensity and negative density
typical_exposure_times.UV_lamp_365nm: 5-30 minutes depending on lamp power and distance

exposure_and_processing.development: Wash print in running water for 5-10 minutes until unexposed areas clear (yellow-green sensitizer washes out leaving white paper)
exposure_and_processing.intensification_optional: H2O2 bath darkens the blue by oxidizing any remaining Fe(II) to Fe(III) in the Prussian blue structure
exposure_and_processing.toning_optional: Various chemical treatments can shift the blue to brown/black (e.g., tannic acid, sodium carbonate, ammonia)

section_5_prussian_blue_chemistry.overview: Prussian blue (普鲁士蓝) is the first modern synthetic pigment, discovered accidentally in Berlin around 1706 by Diesbach. It is a mixed-valence iron(III) hexacyanoferrate(II) compound.
section_5_prussian_blue_chemistry.composition: Fe4[Fe(CN)6]3 · xH2O ('insoluble' form) or KFe[Fe(CN)6] · xH2O ('soluble' form - actually colloidal)
section_5_prussian_blue_chemistry.color_origin: Intense blue color arises from intervalence charge-transfer (IVCT) transition: electron transfer from Fe(II) to Fe(III) via the cyanide bridge, absorbing red light (~700nm)
section_5_prussian_blue_chemistry.structure: Face-centered cubic framework of Fe(II)-C≡N-Fe(III) units with Fe(II) octahedrally coordinated by carbon ends of cyanide and Fe(III) octahedrally coordinated by nitrogen ends. Water molecules occupy the interstitial sites.
section_5_prussian_blue_chemistry.chemical_sponge_property: Prussian blue acts as a 'chemical sponge' - its open framework structure can sequester cations such as Cs+ and Tl+, making it useful for radioactive cesium removal (e.g., after nuclear accidents) and heavy metal detoxification.
section_5_prussian_blue_chemistry.formation_in_cyanotype: Fe2+ (photoproduced) + K3[Fe(CN)6] → KFe[Fe(CN)6] + 2K+. The KFe[Fe(CN)6] is the 'soluble' Prussian blue form that precipitates as fine colloidal particles on the paper.

cyanotype_teaching_modules.source: Kmet et al. (2023), J. Chem. Educ., doi:10.1021/acs.jchemed.2c01089
cyanotype_teaching_modules.content: Ready-to-use classroom activities linking redox photochemistry, coordination chemistry, and photographic science. Covers ferrioxalate/ferric citrate sensitizers forming Prussian blue.

cyanotype_teaching_modules.source: Olivo & Girelli (2015), J. Chem. Educ., doi:10.1021/ed500809n
cyanotype_teaching_modules.content: Integrated experiment: K3[Fe(C2O4)3] synthesis → paper coating → UV exposure → Prussian blue image. Connects inorganic synthesis, photochemistry, and history of photography.

cyanotype_teaching_modules.source: Morizot et al. (2014), J. Chem. Educ., doi:10.1021/ed5000229
cyanotype_teaching_modules.content: First-year students synthesize Prussian blue pigment AND create cyanotype images. Links inorganic synthesis, redox photochemistry, and artistic dimensions of early photography.

cyanotype_teaching_modules.source: Lawrence & Fishelson (1999), J. Chem. Educ., doi:10.1021/ed076p1216A
cyanotype_teaching_modules.content: Two-part demonstration combining cyanotype blueprinting with sunscreen UV-catalysis experiment. Connects photochemistry to everyday consumer products.
Simple, fast, facile demonstration of photochemical redox reaction using visible light. Suitable for introductory chemistry courses.

redox_potentials.delta_E: 0.458 V (O2 can oxidize Fe2+ at any pH)

melanterite_structure.seventh_water: One interstitial H2O not coordinated to any metal, held only by H-bonds (Type 2 water)
melanterite_structure.H_bond_network: Links M1 octahedra to M2 octahedra via sulfate tetrahedra, forming undulating layers of SO4-M1-SO4-M2 polyhedra

cyanotype_quantum_yield.ferrioxalate_actinometer: Standard chemical actinometer calibrated by Hatchard-Parker (1956), IUPAC Technical Report (2004)

mohr_salt_stability.title: 关于硫酸亚铁铵稳定性的讨论
mohr_salt_stability.key_contribution: Systematic refutation of void theory and H-bond theory; proposes water classification theory as correct explanation for Mohr's salt stability
mohr_salt_stability.key_data: H-bond energy comparison (64 vs 84 kJ/mol); per-Fe molar volume comparison (206 vs 146 cm3/mol); Type 1/2/3 water classification

mohr_salt_stability.title: 摩尔盐的空气稳定性 (冯博 等)
mohr_salt_stability.key_contribution: Crystal structure comparison of melanterite vs mohrite; bond distance calculations for O2 penetration analysis; solution vs crystal stability comparison; metal passivation analogy
mohr_salt_stability.key_data: Crystal lattice parameters; interatomic distances in both crystals

mohr_salt_stability.title: 硫酸亚铁铵和硫酸亚铁稳定性的对比研究 (李俊生等, 化学教育 2014年第7期)
mohr_salt_stability.key_contribution: Comparative study of FeSO4 and Mohr's salt stability (cited in the 关于稳定性讨论 paper as propounding the 'hydrogen bond theory')

cyanotype_fundamentals.title: The Photochemical Basis of Cyanotype Photography
cyanotype_fundamentals.key_contribution: Clarifies that near-UV light photoreduces Fe(III) in ammonium iron(III) citrate to Fe(II), which reduces ferricyanide to precipitate Prussian blue

cyanotype_fundamentals.title: Prussian Blue: Artists' Pigment and Chemists' Sponge
cyanotype_fundamentals.key_contribution: Comprehensive review of Prussian blue: discovery, pigment use, cyanotype formation, electronic structure, IVCT color, cation sequestration

cyanotype_fundamentals.title: The Cyanotype Process and Its Potential in Chemistry Education
cyanotype_fundamentals.key_contribution: Modern educational framework for cyanotype; ready-to-use classroom activities

cyanotype_fundamentals.title: Blueprint Photography by the Cyanotype Process
cyanotype_fundamentals.key_contribution: Demonstration combining cyanotype with sunscreen UV absorption experiment

cyanotype_fundamentals.title: Introducing the Human Element in Chemistry by Synthesizing Blue Pigments and Creating Cyanotypes
cyanotype_fundamentals.key_contribution: First-year chemistry: synthesize Prussian blue + create cyanotype images

cyanotype_fundamentals.title: A New Approach toward Cyanotype Photography Using Tris-(oxalato)ferrate(III): An Integrated Experiment
cyanotype_fundamentals.key_contribution: K3[Fe(C2O4)3] synthesis → cyanotype printing integrated experiment

cyanotype_fundamentals.title: A Photographic Process Using Easily Available Reagents
cyanotype_fundamentals.key_contribution: Simplified cyanotype process with accessible reagents

mohr_salt_key_insights: The widely-cited 'void theory' (空隙说) for Mohr's salt stability is based on a critical calculation error: using S6+ ionic radius (0.029nm) instead of the correct SO42- thermochemical radius (0.230nm). When corrected, O2 is too large to pass through crystal voids in EITHER salt.
The 'hydrogen bond network theory' (氢键说) also fails: FeSO4·7H2O actually has stronger total H-bond energy (84 kJ/mol from 4 O-H···:O bonds) than Mohr's salt (64 kJ/mol from 8 N-H···:O bonds).

The correct explanation is the WATER CLASSIFICATION THEORY: FeSO4·7H2O has one 'Type 2' water molecule held ONLY by hydrogen bonds (no metal coordination), which can easily be lost. This creates surface defects that expose [Fe(H2O)6]2+ to O2. Mohr's salt has all 6 waters coordinated to Fe2+ -- no weak Type 2 water.

In SOLUTION, both salts are equally oxidation-prone. Apparent differences may arise from ionic strength effects (~1.75× higher for Mohr's salt at equal concentration), not from any inherent chemical stability difference.
The standard preservation method for BOTH solutions requires: (a) ~0.18M H2SO4 (pH≈0.74), (b) fresh preparation before use, (c) iron nail in reagent bottle.
cyanotype_key_insights: Two main sensitizer systems exist: (1) Classic: ammonium iron(III) citrate + K3[Fe(CN)6]; (2) Ferrioxalate: K3[Fe(C2O4)3]·3H2O (± K3[Fe(CN)6]).
The photochemical basis is consistent: light-driven LMCT photoreduces Fe(III) to Fe(II), which then reduces [Fe(CN)6]3- to Prussian blue.
Prussian blue's color is an intervalence charge-transfer phenomenon: electron hopping between Fe(II) and Fe(III) via cyanide bridges absorbs red light.
Prussian blue acts as a 'chemical sponge' for cations (Cs+, Tl+), relevant to environmental remediation.
Cyanotype is an ideal integrated teaching platform linking: inorganic synthesis → coordination chemistry → photochemistry → redox chemistry → art history.
